steps.title: Identify the contrast being signaled
steps.content: The sentence describes two related but distinct ideas: (1) the novel originally had a different title, and (2) Dickens had planned to call it Mag's Diversions while writing/editing. A transition word is needed to signal how these relate. Since the second statement clarifies or adds to the first (rather than contradicting it directly), the transition word though — meaning “granted that” or “admittedly” — is the right choice.

steps.title: Match the punctuation pattern for parenthetical transitions
steps.content:
When a transition word like though interrupts a sentence as a parenthetical remark, it must be set off by commas on both sides: "...title, though, while writing..." This pattern is standard in English. The comma after though allows the dependent clause "while writing and editing" to clearly modify the following independent clause "Dickens had planned."

Choice A places a semicolon after though, which incorrectly tries to separate two independent clauses when a dependent clause follows. Choices C and D place the transition word directly before "while" without a comma after though, breaking the parenthetical pattern.

That matches choice B — B is correct.
